text: The laparoscopic view reveals a large subserous fibroid located at the fundus of the uterus. The surgical procedure is conducted without the use of a uterine manipulator.
The surgeon injects vasopressin into the uterine tissue to reduce blood flow and devascularize the area prior to fibroid removal.
The surgeon makes an oblique incision on the anterior surface of the large subserous fundal uterine fibroid, followed by the enucleation of the fibroid from the surrounding uterine tissue.
The surgeon removes a small fibroid and sutures the inner layer of the defect using a continuous V-lock barb suture.
The surgeon sutures the cirrhosa of the uterus using interrupted sutures, showing the appearance of the uterus after the completion of suturing.
The surgeon makes an incision on another subserous fibroid and enucleates it. The resulting defect is sutured in one layer using interrupted sutures.
A surgical bag is introduced into the abdominal cavity, and the fibroids are placed inside. The fibroids are then morcellated within the bag, reducing their size for removal.
The surgeon applies anti-adhesion gel over the incisions on the uterus, covering the areas where fibroids were previously removed and sutured.
